skill.name: mulerouter
skill.description: Generate and edit images or videos through MuleRouter and MuleRun multimodal APIs using command-line model wrappers.
skill.summary: Runs MuleRouter and MuleRun image, video, and editing models from CLI workflows.
skill.author: openmule

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical heuristic because this skill combines local Python execution, outbound network calls, and API credential access. Manual review found no evidence of malicious exfiltration or prompt injection; the confirmed risk is expected third-party API use with bearer credentials and user-supplied media prompts. Publish with clear warnings about external API transmission and local credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated requests send user inputs to external APIs
medium_findings.description: The client builds an Authorization bearer header from the configured API key and sends request bodies to MuleRouter or MuleRun endpoints. This is core functionality, but users must understand that prompts, media URLs, and task data leave the local environment.
medium_findings.recommendation: Warn users that prompt text, image URLs, audio URLs, and API credentials are used with MuleRouter or MuleRun services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bearer token header and HTTP request call are directly visible. The behavior is legitimate for this API skill, but it is still a real data-sharing boundary.

medium_findings.title: Environment and .env credential loading
medium_findings.description: The configuration loader imports python-dotenv, loads a provided file or current-directory .env file, and reads MULEROUTER_SITE and MULEROUTER_API_KEY. This is expected credential configuration, but it can expose credentials to the skill process.
medium_findings.recommendation: Use a dedicated API key with limited scope and avoid running the skill in directories with unrelated .env secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment loading and API key lookup are explicit. I did not find evidence that unrelated environment variables are sent over the network.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic local module loading for model registration
medium_findings.description: The model listing workflow executes local provider package files through importlib so endpoints can register themselves. The provider list is fixed in the repository, so this is not arbitrary user-controlled import, but it is still local code execution.
medium_findings.recommendation: Keep the fixed allowlist of model files and review any new provider files before publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The importlib exec_module call is present in the model listing script. The provider names are hardcoded rather than supplied by user input.

low_findings.title: Markdown command examples are not runtime command execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged many fenced code examples and inline command references as shell execution. These are documentation examples in Markdown, not code paths that execute commands automatically.
low_findings.recommendation: No code change is required for the documentation examples, but marketplace copy should make clear that users run commands explicitly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown documentation blocks and inline examples. No runtime shell invocation is present at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are string false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm cluster appears to match ordinary words such as description, model names, version strings, and seed parameters. I did not find cryptographic hashing, encryption, or signature code in the inspected implementation.
low_findings.recommendation: Treat these weak-crypto detections as false positives unless future code adds real cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptions and model parameters, not crypto APIs. No evidence of weak cryptographic usage was found in reviewed core files.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance alerts map to task metadata parsing
low_findings.description: The low system reconnaissance alerts correspond to task response fields and model listing output, not host discovery or operating system inspection. No evidence found of system inventory collection.
low_findings.recommendation: No action is needed for these detections; keep task IDs and status output limited to API workflow data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines parse API task IDs and optional site configuration. They do not enumerate files, users, processes, network interfaces, or host properties.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-backed outbound HTTP client
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill creates an httpx client with a bearer token and posts model request bodies to configured external API endpoints. This is expected behavior but requires user consent and secret handling guidance.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Publish with a warning that generated media requests use third-party services and may include user-provided URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP client and POST/GET helpers are direct evidence. The target base URLs are limited by Config to MuleRouter or MuleRun.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-local dynamic imports
dangerous_patterns.description: The model registry discovery path executes fixed local Python modules during provider discovery. This is controlled by repository-maintained package imports, not direct user input.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Keep model imports allowlisted and avoid globbing arbitrary files from writable directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic import concern is confirmed in scripts/list_models.py, but the source package list is constrained to hardcoded local providers.

content.value_statement: Creative teams need repeatable access to image and video models without rebuilding API calls. This skill wraps MuleRouter and MuleRun models in CLI workflows for generation, editing, polling, and result retrieval.
actual_capabilities: Lists available MuleRouter and MuleRun models with provider and output filters.
Generates images from text prompts using supported image model endpoints.
Creates videos from text prompts or image URLs using supported video endpoints.
Supports image editing, image-to-image, VACE, and keyframe video workflows.
Polls asynchronous tasks and returns task IDs, statuses, and result URLs.
limitations: Requires Python 3.10+, dependencies, and network access to MuleRouter or MuleRun.
Requires a valid MULEROUTER_SITE value and MuleRouter API key.
Sends prompts, media URLs, and task metadata to external API services.
Model availability, cost, latency, and policy enforcement are controlled by the API provider.

use_cases.title: Prototype campaign visuals
use_cases.description: Generate image variations and short videos from approved creative prompts for early concept review.

use_cases.title: Automate media model testing
use_cases.description: List model parameters, run sample requests, and compare returned task results across providers.

use_cases.title: Prepare social video drafts
use_cases.description: Turn text prompts or source images into short video outputs with reproducible model settings.

prompt_templates.title: List available models
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the MuleRouter skill to show available models for my configured site. Summarize which ones create images and which ones create videos.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image with Nano Banana Pro using this prompt: [describe the scene]. Use a square aspect ratio and return the result URL.

prompt_templates.title: Create a short video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a five second text-to-video result with Wan2.6. Use this prompt: [describe motion, setting, and subject]. Wait for completion.

prompt_templates.title: Compare model options
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the available image-to-video and video editing models. Recommend one for [goal], explain required parameters, then run it after I confirm.

output_examples.input: List video models available for MuleRun.
output_examples.output: A grouped model summary showing Alibaba video endpoints, supported actions, and the total number of matching endpoints.

output_examples.input: Generate a mountain lake image for a concept board.
output_examples.output: A completed task report with the task status and one or more image result URLs.

output_examples.input: Animate this source image with a slow camera push.
output_examples.output: A video task result that includes the task ID, completion status, and generated video URL.

best_practices: Use a dedicated MuleRouter API key and avoid sharing it in prompts or logs.
Run list-params before using a new model so required options are clear.
Use explicit prompts, aspect ratios, durations, and seeds when repeatability matters.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill in a directory that contains unrelated production .env secrets.
Do not send confidential images, audio, prompts, or URLs unless the API policy allows it.
Do not assume all documented models are available on both MuleRouter and MuleRun.

faq.question: Does this skill run models locally?
faq.answer: No. It calls MuleRouter or MuleRun APIs and returns task results from those services.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: It requires MULEROUTER_SITE and MULEROUTER_API_KEY through environment variables, a .env file, or CLI options.

faq.question: Can it generate both images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The included endpoints cover text-to-image, text-to-video, image-to-video, image editing, and video editing workflows.

faq.question: Can I see model parameters before running a request?
faq.answer: Yes. Each model script supports parameter listing before you submit a generation or editing task.

faq.question: Does it wait for long-running tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. The shared workflow can poll asynchronous tasks until completion or return a task ID without waiting.

faq.question: Is it safe for private media?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and media URLs are sent to external APIs, so follow your organization policy before submitting sensitive content.
